entities.name: Careerspan
entities.context: V's company; product design should focus on reducing activation energy of story extraction

entities.name: inertia of self-reflection
entities.context: The high cognitive and emotional cost that prevents proactive engagement with career development until external pressure forces action

entities.name: hyperbolic discounting
entities.context: Behavioral economics principle explaining why self-reflection (delayed rewards) is deprioritized versus immediate tactical demands

entities.name: activation energy
entities.context: Metaphor for the friction/effort required to begin narrative extraction; product design goal is to reduce this

entities.name: readiness inertia
entities.context: State where users remain disengaged regardless of tool value due to lack of immediate pressure

entities.name: narrative extraction
entities.context: The process of extracting one's professional story; high-cost activity that tools should make easier

entities.name: micro-tasks
entities.context: Design approach to break reflection into small pieces that don't trigger cognitive avoidance

entities.name: high-stakes proxy
entities.context: Feature approach that simulates pressure of high-stakes environments to overcome inertia

relationships.context: Core problem Careerspan must solve in product design
Hyperbolic discounting explains why self-reflection is avoided - delayed rewards are undervalued
Product design should focus on reducing activation energy of story extraction
Micro-tasks reduce activation energy by avoiding triggering cognitive avoidance
Simulating high-stakes pressure can overcome readiness inertia
High cost of narrative extraction is the source of reflection inertia
